24.36: And Sara my lord's wife hath born my lord a son in her old age, and he hath given him all things that he had.
24.37: And my lord adjured me saying: Thou shalt not take a wife for my son of the Chananites, in whose land I dwell:
24.38: but thou shalt go to my father's house, and of mine own kindred shalt thou take a wife for my son:
24.39: but I answered my lord: What if the woman will not come with me?
24.40: Our Lord, saith he, in whose sight I walk, will send his Angel with thee, & will direct thy way: and thou shalt take a wife for my son of mine own kindred, and of my father's house.
24.41: Thou shalt be innocent from my curse, when thou shalt come to my kin, and they will not give her thee.
24.42: I came therefore today to the well of water, and said: O Lord God of my lord Abraham, if thou hast directed my way, wherein I now walk,
24.43: behold I stand besides the well of water, and the virgin, that shall come forth to draw water, when she shall hear me say: Give me a little water to drink of thy tankard;
24.44: and she shall say to me: Drink both thou and for thy camels I will draw also; that is the woman, which our Lord hath prepared for my master's son.
24.45: And whilst I pondered these things secretly with myself, Rebecca appeared coming with a tankard, which she carried upon her shoulder: & she went down to the fountain, & drew water.
24.46: And I said to her: Give me a little to drink. Who speedily let down the tankard from her shoulder, and said to me: Drink Both thou, and to thy camels I will give drink. I drank, and she watered the camels.
24.47: And I asked her, and said: Whose daughter art thou? Who answered: I am the daughter of Bathuel, the son of Nachor, whom Melcha bare him. I hung therefore earlets to adorn her face, and I put bracelets upon her hands.
24.48: And prostrate I adored our Lord, blessing the Lord God of my lord Abraham, who hath brought me the straight way to take the daughter of my lord's brother for his son.
24.49: Wherefore if you do according to mercy and truth with my lord, shew me: but if it please you otherwise, that also tell me, that I may go to the right hand or to the left.
24.50: And Laban and Bathuel answered: From our Lord the word hath proceeded: we cannot speak any other thing with thee besides his pleasure.
24.51: Behold Rebecca is before thee, take her and go thy ways, & let her be the wife of thy lord's son, as our Lord hath spoken.
24.52: Which when Abraham's servant heard, falling down he adored our Lord to the ground.
24.53: And taking forth vessels of silver, and gold, and garments, gave them to Rebecca for a present. To her brothers also, and to her mother he offered gifts.
24.54: Abanquet was made, and eating and drinking together they lodged there. And in the morning the servant arose, and said: Dismiss me, that I may go to my lord.
24.55: And her brother and mother answered: Let the maid tarry at the least ten days with us, & after she shall depart.
24.56: Stay me not, said he, because our Lord hath directed my way: dismiss me that I may go on to my lord.
24.57: And they said: Let us call the maid, and ask her will.
24.58: And being called, when she was come, they asked: Wilt thou go with this man? Who said: I will go.
24.59: They dismissed her therefore, and her nurse, and Abraham's servant, and his company,
24.60: wishing prosperity to their sister, and saying: Thou art our sister, increase thou into thousand thousands, and thy seed possess the gates of their enemies.
24.61: Therefore Rebecca and her maids being set upon camels, followed the man: who with speed returned unto his lord; and
24.62: the same time Isaac walked along the way, that leadeth to the well of the Living and the seeing, so called; for he dwelt in the south country:
24.63: and he was gone forth to meditate in the field, the day now being well spent: and when he head cast up his eyes, he saw camels coming a far off.
24.64: Rebecca also, when she saw Isaac, lighted off the camel,
24.65: and said to the servant: Who is that man which cometh towards us along the field? And he said to her: The same is my lord. But she quickly taking her cloak, covered herself.
24.66: And the servant told Isaac all things that he had done.
24.67: Who brought her into the tent of Sara his mother, & took her to wife: and he loved her so much, that it did moderate the sorrow which was chanced of his mother's death.

25.1: And Abraham married another wife named Cetura:
25.2: Which bare him Zamran, and Jecsan, and Madan, and Madian, and Jesboc, and Sue.
25.3: Jecsan also begat Saba & Dadan. The Children of Dadan were Assurim, and Latusim, and Loomim.
25.4: But also of Madian was born Epha, and Opher, and Henoch, and Abida, and Eldaa: all these were the children of Cetura.
25.5: And Abraham gave all his possessions to Isaac:
25.6: and to the children of his concubines he gave gifts, and separated them from Isaac his son, whilst himself yet lived, to the east country.
25.7: And the days of Abraham's life were an hundred seventy and five years.
25.8: And decaying died in a good old age, and having lived a great time, and being full of days and was gathered to his people.
25.9: And there buried him Isaac and Ismael his sons in the double cave, which was situated in the field of Ephron the son of Seor the Hethite, over-against Mambre,
25.10: which he had bought of the children of Heth: there was he buried, and Sara his wife.
25.11: And after his death God blessed Isaac his son, who dwelled beside the well of the Living & Seeing, so named.
25.12: These are the generations of Ismael the son of Abraham, whom Agar the Ægyptian bare him, Sara's servant: &
25.13: These are the names of his children according to their calling and generations. The first begotten of Ishmael Nabajoth, then Cedar, and Adbeel, and Mabsam,
25.14: Masma also, and Duma, and Massa,
25.15: Hadar, and Thema, and Iethur, and Naphis, and Cedma.
25.16: These are the sons of Ismael: and these are their names by their castles and towns, twelve Princes of their tribes.
25.17: And the years of Ismael's life came to an hundred thirty seven, and decaying died, and was put unto his people.
25.18: And he dwelt from Hevila even to Sur, which looketh towards Ægypt, as they enter to the Assyrians, before the face of all his brethren died he.
25.19: These also are the generations of Isaac the son of Abraham: Abraham begat Isaac:
25.20: Who when he was forty years old, took to wife Rebecca the daughter of Bathuel the Syrian of Mesopotamia, sister to Laban.
25.21: And Isaac besought our Lord for his wife, because she was barren: who heard him, and made Rebecca to conceive.
25.22: But the little ones struggled in her womb; who said: If it should be so with me, what need was there to conceive? And she went to consult our Lord.
25.23: Who answering said: Two nations are in thy womb, and two peoples shall be divided out of thy womb, and one people shall overcome the other, and the elder shall serve the younger.
25.24: Now her time was come to be delivered, and behold twins were found in her womb.
25.25: He that came forth first, was red, and all hairy in manner of a skin: and his name was called Esau. Immediately the other coming forth, held his brother's plant in his hand: and therefore he called him Jacob.
25.26: Threescore years old was Isaac, when the little ones were born unto him.
25.27: Who being grown up, Esau became a man cunning in hunting, & a husband man: but Jacob a plain man dwelled in tents:
25.28: Isaac loved Esau, because he did eat of his hunting: and Rebecca loved Jacob.
25.29: & Jacob boiled broth: to whom Esau being come faint out of the field,
25.30: said: Give me of this red broth, because I am exceeding faint. For which cause his name was called Edom.
25.31: To whom Jacob said: sell me thy first-birth-right.
25.32: He answered, Lo I die, what will the first-birth-right avail me?
25.33: Jacob said: Swear therefore to me. Esau sware to him, and sold his first-birth-right.
25.34: And so taking bread and the rice broth, did eat, and drink, and went his way; little esteeming that he had sold his first-birth-right.

26.1: And when a famine was risen in the land, after that sterility that had chanced in the days of Abraham, Isaac went to Abimelech king of the Palestines into Gerara.
26.2: And our Lord appeared to him, and said: Go not down into Ægypt, but rest in the land which I shall tell thee.
26.3: And sojourn in it, and I will be with thee, and will bless thee: for to thee and to thy seed I will give all these countries, accomplishing the oath which I sware to Abraham thy father.
26.4: And I will multiply thy seed as the stars of heaven: and I will give to thy posterity all these countries: and in thy seed Shall be Blessed All The Nations of The Earth,
26.5: for because Abraham obeyed my voice, & kept my precepts and commandments, and observed my ceremonies and laws.
26.7: Who when he was asked by the men of that place concerning his wife, answered: She is my sister; for he was afraid to confess that she was married to him, thinking lest peradventure they would kill him because of her beauty.
26.8: And when very many days were passed, & he abode there, Abimelech the king of the Palestines looking forth through a window saw him sporting with Rebecca his wife.
26.9: And calling for him, he said: it is evident that she is thy wife: why didst thou feign her to be thy sister? He answered: I feared lest I should die for her.
26.10: And Abimelech said: Why hast thou deceived us? Some man of the people might have lien with thy wife, and thou hadst brought upon us a great sin. And he commanded all the people, saying:
26.11: He that shall touch this man's wife, dying shall die.
26.12: And Isaac sowed in that land, and he found that same year and hundred fold: and our Lord blessed him.
26.13: And the man was made rich, and he went prospering and increasing, till he was made exceeding great:
26.14: and he had also possessions of sheep and of herds, and a very great family. For this the Palestines envying him,
26.15: stopped at that time all the wells, that the servants of his father Abraham had digged, filling them up with earth:
26.16: in so much that Abimelech himself said to Isaac: Depart from us, because thou art become mightier than we a great deal.
26.17: And departing, to come to the Torrent of Gerara, and to dwell there,
26.18: again he digged other wells, which the servants of his father Abraham had digged, and which after his death the Philistines had stopped up of old: & he called them by the same names which his father before had called them.
26.19: And they digged in the Torrent, and found living water:
26.20: but there also the pastors of Gerara made a brawl against the pastors of Isaac, saying: it is our water. For which cause he called the name of the well, by occasion of that which had happened, Calumny.
26.21: And they digged also another; and for that they brawled likewise, and he called the name of it, Enmity.
26.22: Going forward from thence he digged another well, for which they contended not: therefore he called the name thereof, Latitude, saying: Now hath our Lord dilated us, and made us to increase upon the earth.
26.23: And he went up from that place unto Bersabee,
26.24: Where our Lord appeared to him that same night, saying: I am the God of Abraham thy father, do not fear, because I am with thee: I will bless thee, and multiply thy seed for my servant Abraham's sake.
26.25: Therefore he builded there an altar: and having called upon the name of our Lord, he pitched his tent: & commanded his servants that they should dig a well.
26.26: To the which place when there were come from Gerara Abimelech, and Ocozath his friend, & Phicol chief Captain of his soldiers,
26.27: Isaac spake to them: Why are ye come to me a man whom you hated, and have thrust out from you?
26.28: Who answered: We saw that the Lord is with thee, and therefore we said: Let there be an oath between us, and let us make a league,
26.29: that thou do us no harm, as we also have touched nothing of thine, neither have we done that which might hurt thee: but with peace have we dismissed thee increased with the blessing of the Lord.
26.30: Therefore he made them a feast, and after they had eaten and drunken,
26.31: Arising in the morning, they sware one to another: and Isaac dismissed them peaceably into their place.
26.32: And behold the same day came the servants of Isaac telling him of a well, which they had digged, and saying: We have found water.
26.33: Whereupon he called it Abundance: and the name of the city was given Bersabee, even unto this present day.
26.34: But Esau being forty years old married wives, Judith the daughter of Beeri the Hethite, and Basemath the daughter of Elon of the same place:
26.35: Both which had offended the mind of Isaac and Rebecca.

27.1: And Isaac was old, and his eyes were dim, and he could not see: and he called Esau his elder son, and said to him: My son? Who answered: Here I am.
27.2: To whom his father: Thou seest, quoth he, that I am old, and know not the day of my death.
27.3: Take thy instruments, thy quiver, and bow, and go abroad: and when thou hast taken any thing by hunting,
27.4: make me broth thereof, as thou knowest I like, and bring that I may eat, and my soul may bless thee before I die.
27.5: Which when Rebecca had heard, and he was gone into the field to fulfill his father's commandment,
27.6: she said to her son Jacob: I heard thy father talking with Esau thy brother, and saying to him:
27.7: Bring me of thy hunting, and make me meats that I may eat, and bless thee in the sight of our Lord before I die.
27.8: Now therefore, my son, assent to my counsel:
27.9: and go thy way to the flock, bring me two kids of the best, that I may make of them meat for thy father, such as he gladly eateth:
27.10: Which when thou hast brought in, and he hath eaten, he may bless thee before he die.
27.11: To whom he answered: Thou knowest that Esau my brother is an hairy man, and I am smooth:
27.12: if my father shall feel me, and perceive it, I fear lest he will think I would have deluded him, and I shall bring upon me a curse for a blessing.
27.13: To whom his mother said: This curse, my son, light upon me, only hear thou my voice, and go, fetch me the things which I have said.
27.14: He went, and brought, and gave them to his mother. She dressed meats, even as she knew his father liked.
27.15: And she did on him the garments of Esau very good, which she had at home with her:
27.16: and the little skins of the kids she put about his hands, and covered the bare of his neck.
27.17: And she gave him the broth, and delivered him bread that she had baked.
27.18: Which when he had carried in, he said: My father? But he answered: I hear. Who art thou my son?
27.19: And Jacob said: I am thy first begotten Esau: I have done as thou didst command me: arise, sit, and eat of my hunting, that thy soul may bless me.
27.20: And again Isaac to his son: How couldest thou, said he, find it so quickly, my son? Who answered: It was the will of God that that which I would came quickly in my way.
27.21: And Isaac said: Come hither, that I may feel thee my son, and may prove whether thou be my son Esau, or not.
27.22: He came near to his father, and when he had felt him, Isaac said: The voice verily, is the voice of Jacob: but the hands are the hands of Esau.
27.23: And he knew him not, because his hairy hands had made him like unto the elder. Blessing him therefore,
27.24: he said: Art thou my son Esau? He answered: I am.
27.25: But he said: Bring me the meats of thy hunting, my son, that my soul may bless thee. Which when they were brought and he had eaten, he offered him wine also, which after he had drunk,
27.26: he said to him: Come near me, and give me a kiss, my son.
27.27: He came near, and kissed him. And immediately as he felt the fragrant savour of his garments, blessing him, he said: Behold the savour of my son is as the savour of a plentiful field, which our Lord hath blessed.
27.28: God give thee of the dew of heaven, & of the fatness of the earth abundance of corn and wine.
27.29: And let peoples serve thee, and tribes adore thee: be thou lord of thy brethren, and thy mother's children bow they before thee. He that shall curse thee, be he cursed: and he that shall bless thee, be he replenished with blessings.
27.30: Isaac had scarce ended his words, and Jacob now gone forth abroad, but Esau came,
27.31: and brought in to his father meats made of his hunting, saying: Arise my father, and eat of thy son's hunting; that thy soul may bless me.
27.32: And Isaac said to him: Why! Who art thou? Who answered: I am thy first begotten son Esau.
27.33: Isaac was amazed and astonished exceedingly, and marveling more than a man can believe, said: Who is he then that even now brought me venison that he had taken, and I did eat of all things before thou camest, and I have blessed him, and he shall be blessed.
27.34: Esau having heard his father's words, roared out with a great cry; and being dismayed, said: Bless me also, my father.
27.35: Who said: Thy brother came deceitfully and took thy blessing.
27.36: But he said again: Rightly is his name called Jacob; for he hath supplanted me lo the second time: my first-birth-right he took before, and now the second time he hath stolen my blessing. And again to his father he said: Hast thou not reserved me also a blessing?
27.37: Isaac answered: I have appointed him thy lord, and all his brethren I have made subject to his service: with corn and wine I have established him, and for thee, my son, what shall I do more after this?
27.38: To whom Esau said: Hast thou one only blessing, father? I beseech thee bless me also. And when he wept that he howled again,
27.39: Isaac being moved, said to him: In the fat of the earth, and in the dew of heaven from above,
27.40: shall thy blessing be. Thou shalt live by the sword, and shalt serve thy brother: and the time shall come, when thou shalt shake off and loose his yoke from thy neck.
27.41: Esau therefore always hated Jacob for the blessing wherewith his father had blessed him; and he said in his heart: The days will come of the mourning of my father, and I will kill Jacob my brother.
27.42: These things were told to Rebecca: who sending and calling Jacob her son, said to him: Behold Esau thy brother threateneth to kill thee.
27.43: Now therefore, my son, hear my voice, and get thee up and fly to Laban my brother into Haran:
27.44: and thou shalt dwell with him a few days, till the fury of thy brother be assuaged,
27.45: and his indignation cease, and he forget those things which thou hast done to him: afterward I will send, and bring thee from thence hither. Why shall I be deprived of both sons in one day?
27.46: And Rebecca said to Isaac: I am weary of my life for the daughters of Heth: if Jacob take a wife of the stock of this land, I list not live.

28.1: Isaac therefore called Jacob, and blessed him, and commanded him saying: Take not a wife of the stock of Chanaan;
28.2: but go and make a journey into Mesopotamia of Syria; to the house of Bathuel thy mother's father, and take thee a wife thence of the daughters of Laban thine uncle.
28.3: And God Almighty bless thee, and make thee increase, and multiply thee: that thou mayest be into multitudes of peoples.
28.4: And give he thee the blessings of Abraham, and to thy seed after thee: that thou mayest possess the land of thy peregrination, which he promised to thy grandfather.
28.5: And when Isaac had dismissed him, taking his journey he came to Mesopotamia of Syria to Laban, the son of Bathuel the Syrian, brother to Rebecca his mother.
28.6: And Esau seeing that his father had blessed Jacob, and had sent him into Mesopotamia of Syria, to marry a wife thence; and that after the blessing he had commanded him, saying: Thou shalt not take a wife of the daughters of Chanaan;
28.7: and that Jacob obeying his parents was gone into Syria:
28.8: having trial also that his father did not willingly see the daughters of Chanaan,
28.9: he went to Ismael, and took to wife besides them which he had before, Maheleth the daughter of Ismael Abraham's son, sister to Nabajoth.
28.10: Therefore Jacob being departed from Bersabee, went on to Haran.
28.11: And when he was come to a certain place, and would rest in it after sun set, he took one of the stones that lay there, and putting it under his head, slept in the same place.
28.12: And he saw in his sleep a ladder standing upon the earth, & the top thereof touching heaven: the Angels also of God ascending & descending by it,
28.13: and our Lord leaning upon the ladder saying to him: I am the Lord God of Abraham thy father, & the God of Isaac: the Land wherein thou sleepest, I will give to thee and to thy seed.
28.14: And thy seed shall be as the dust of the earth: thou shalt be dilated to the West, and to the East, and to the North, and to the South: and IN Thee And Thy Seed All The Tribes of The Earth Shall be Blessed.
28.15: And Iwill be thy keeper whither soever thou goest, and will bring thee back into this land: neither will I leave thee, till I shall have accomplished all things which I have said.
28.16: And when Jacob was awaked out of sleep, he said: Indeed our Lord is in this place, and I wist not.
28.17: And trembling he said: How terrible is this place! This is none other but the house of God, and the gate of heaven.
28.18: And Jacob arising in the morning took the stone, which he had laid under his head, and erected it for a title, pouring oil upon the top.
28.19: And he called the name of the city Bethel, which before was called Luza.
28.20: And he vowed a vow, saying: If God shall be with me, and shall keep me in the way, by the which I walk, and shall give me bread to eat, and raiment to put on,
28.21: and I shall be returned prosperously to my father's house, the Lord shall be my God,
28.22: and this stone, which I have erected for a title, shall be called the House of God: and of all things that thou shalt give to me, I will offer tithes to thee.

29.1: Jacob therefore going on his journey, came into the East country.
29.2: And he saw a well in the field, and three flocks of sheep lying beside it: for of it the beasts were watered, and the mouth thereof was closed with a great stone.
29.3: And the manner was when all the sheep were gathered together they did roll off the stone, and after the sheep were refreshed they put it on the mouth of the well again.
29.4: And he said to the shepherds: Brethren, whence are you? Who answered: Of Haran.
29.5: And he asked them, saying: Know you Laban the son of Nachor? They said: We do know him.
29.6: Is he in health? Quoth he: He is in health, say they: And behold Rachel his daughter cometh with his flock.
29.7: And Jacob said: There is yet much day remaining, neither is it time to bring the flocks into the folds again: first give the sheep drink, and so bring them back to feed.
29.8: Who answered: we cannot, till all the cattle be gathered together: and we remove the stone from the well's mouth, that we may water the flocks.
29.9: They were yet speaking, and behold Rachel came with her father's sheep: for she fed the flock.
29.10: Whom when Jacob had seen, & knew her to be his cousin-german, & that they were the sheep of Laban his uncle, he removed the stone, wherewith the well was closed.
29.11: And having watered the flock, he kissed her: and lifting up his voice wept,
29.12: and he told her that he was her father's brother, and the son of Rebecca: but she in haste went & told her father.
29.13: Who when he heard that Jacob his sister's son was come, he ran forth to meet him, & embracing him & heartily kissing him, brought him into his house. And when he had heard the causes of his journey,
29.14: he answered: Thou art my bone and my flesh. And after the days of one month were expired,
29.15: he said to him: because thou art my brother, shalt thou serve me gratis? Tell me, what wages wilt thou take?
29.16: He had indeed two daughters, the name of the elder was Lia; & the younger was called Rachel.
29.17: But Lia was blear-eyed, Rachel well favoured, & of a beautiful countenance.
29.18: Whom Jacob loving, said: I will serve thee for Rachel thy younger daughter seven years.
29.19: Laban answered: It is better that I give her to thee than to another man, tarry with me.
29.20: Jacob therefore served for Rachel seven years: and they seemed a few days because of the greatness of his love.
29.21: And he said to Laban: give me my wife: because now the time is complete, that I may company with her.
29.22: Who having bid a great number of his friends to the feast, made the marriage.
29.23: And at night he brought in Lia his daughter to him,
29.24: giving his daughter a handmaid, named Zelpha. With whom when Jacob had companied after the manner, when morning was come he saw Lia:
29.25: and he said to his father in law: What is it that thou didst mean to do? Did not I serve thee for Rachel? Why hast thou deceived me?
29.26: Laban answered: It is not the custom in this place, that we bestow the younger in marriage first.
29.27: Make up the week of days of this match: and I will give thee this same also, for the work that thou shalt serve me other seven years.
29.28: He yielded to his pleasure; and after the week was past, he married Rachel to wife:
29.29: to whom her father had delivered Bala for to be her servant.
29.30: And having at length obtained the marriage that he wished, he preferred the love of the later before the former, serving with him other seven years.
29.31: And our Lord seeing that he despised Lia, opened her womb, her sister remaining barren.
29.32: Who conceived and bare a son, and called his name Ruben, saying: Our Lord saw mine affliction: now my husband will love me.
29.33: And again she conceived and bare a son, and said: For because our Lord heard that I was contemned, he hath given this also to me: and she called his name Simeon.
29.34: And she conceived the third time, and bare another son: and said: Now also my husband will be joined to me, for because I have born him three sons: and therefore she called his name, Levi.
29.35: The fourth time she conceived and bare a son, and said: Now will I confess to our Lord. And for this she called him Juda: And she left bearing.

30.1: And Rachel seeing she was unfruitful, envied her sister, and said to her husband: Give me children, otherwise I shall die.
30.2: With whom Jacob being angry answered: Am I as God, who hath deprived thee of the fruit of thy womb?
30.3: But she said: I have here my servant Bala: Company with her, that she may bear upon my knees, and I may have children of her.
30.4: And she gave him Bala unto marriage: who,
30.5: When her husband had companied with her, conceived and bare a son.
30.6: And Rachel said: Our Lord hath judged for me and hath heard my voice, giving me a son, and therefore she called his name, Dan.
30.7: And again Bala conceiving bare another,
30.8: for whom Rachel said: God hath compared me with my sister, and I have prevailed: and she called him Nephthali.
30.9: Lia perceiving that she had left bearing, delivered Zelpha her handmaid to her husband.
30.10: Who conceiving and bringing forth a son,
30.11: she said: Happily. And therefore called his name Gad.
30.13: And Lia said: This is for my blessedness: for women will call me blessed. Therefore she called him Aser.
30.14: And Ruben, going forth in the time of wheat harvest into the field, found mandragoras: which he brought to his mother Lia. And Rachel said: Give me part of thy son's mandragoras.
30.15: She answered: Dost thou think it a small matter that thou hast taken my husband from me, unless thou take also my son's mandragoras? Rachel said: For thy son's mandragoras let him sleep with thee this night.
30.16: And when Jacob returned at even from the field, Lia went out to meet him, and said: Company with me, because with wages I have hired thee for my son's mandragoras. And he slept with her that night.
30.17: And God heard her prayers: and she conceived and bare the fifth son,
30.18: And said: God hath given me a reward, because I gave my handmaid to my husband. And she called his name Issachar.
30.19: Again Lia conceiving, bare the sixth son,
30.20: & said: God hath endowed me with a good dowry: this turn also my husband will be with me, for because I have born him six sons, and therefore she called his name, Zabulon.
30.21: After whom she bare a daughter named Dina.
30.22: Our Lord also remembering Rachel, heard her, and opened her womb.
30.23: Who conceived and bare a son, saying: God hath taken away my reproach.
30.24: And she called his name, Joseph, saying: Our Lord add to me another son.
30.25: And when Joseph was born, Jacob said to his father in law: Dismiss me that I may return into my country, and to my land.
30.26: Give me my wives, and my children, for whom I have served thee, that I may depart: thou knowest the service that I have served thee.
30.27: Laban said to him: Let me find grace in thy sight: I have learned by experience that God hath blessed me for thy sake:
30.28: appoint thee wages which I shall give thee.
30.29: But he answered: Thou knowest how I have served thee, and how great thy possession hath been in my hands.
30.30: Thou hadest a small thing before I came to thee, and now thou art made rich: and our Lord hath blessed thee at my coming in. It is reason therefore that once I provide also for mine own house.
30.31: And Laban said: What shall I give thee? But he said: I will nothing: but if thou wilt do that which I demand, I will feed and keep thy sheep again.
30.32: Go round about all the flocks, and separate all the sheep of diverse colours, of speckled fleece: and what-soever shall be russet and spotted, and of diverse colours, as well in the sheep as in the goats, shall be my wages.
30.33: And my justice shall answer for me tomorrow before thee, when the time of the bargain shall come: and all that shall not be of diverse colours, and spotted, and russet, as well in the sheep as in the goats, shall accuse me of theft.
30.34: And Laban said: I like well that thou demandest.
30.35: And he separated the same day the she goats, and the sheep, and the he goats, and the rams of diverse colours, and spotted: and all the flock of one colour, that is of white and black fleece, he delivered in the hand of his sons.
30.36: And he put a space of three days' journey betwixt him and his son in law, who fed the rest of his flock.
30.37: Jacob therefore taking green rods of the poplar, and of the almond, and of the plane-trees, in part pilled them: and when the barks were taken off, in the parts that were pilled there appeared whiteness: but the parts that were whole remained green: and by this means the colour was made diverse.
30.38: And he put them in the troughs, where the water was poured out: that when the flocks should come to drink, they might have the rods before their eyes, and in the sight of them conceive.
30.39: And it came to pass that in the very heat of the ramming, the sheep beheld the rods, and brought forth spotted, and of diverse colours, and speckled.
30.40: And Jacob divided the flock, and put the rods in the troughs before the eyes of the rams: and all the white and the black were Laban's, and the rest, Jacob's, when the flocks were separated one from the other.
30.41: Therefore when the ewes went to ram, in the prime time Jacob put the rods in the troughs of water before the eyes of the rams, and of the ewes, that in looking upon them they might conceive:
30.42: but when the later coming was, and the last conceiving, he did not put them. And those that were lateward became Laban's: and they of the prime time, Jacob's.
30.43: And the man was enriched beyond measure, and he had many flock, women servants and men servants, camels and asses.

31.1: After he heard the words of Laban's sons saying: Jacob hath taken all that was our father's, and being enriched of his substance, is become great:
31.2: and perceiving also Laban's countenance, that it was not towards him as yesterday and the other day,
31.3: especially our Lord saying to him: Return into the land of thy fathers, and to thy kindred, and I will be with thee;
31.4: he sent, and called Rachel and Lia into the field where he fed the flocks,
31.5: and said to them: I see your father's countenance that it is not towards me as yesterday and the other day: and the God of my father hath been with me.
31.6: And yourselves know that I have served your father to the uttermost of my power.
31.7: Yea your father also hath circumvented me, and hath changed my wages ten times: and yet God hath not suffered him to hurt me.
31.8: If at any time he said: They of diverse colours shall be thy wages; all the sheep brought forth young of diverse colours. But when he said contrary: Thou shalt take all the white ones for thy wages; all the flocks brought forth white ones.
31.9: And God hath taken your father's substance, and given it to me.
31.10: For after the time came of the ewes conceiving, I lifted up mine eyes, and saw in my sleep the males ascending upon the females of diverse colours, and the spotted, and the speckled.
31.11: And the Angel of God said to me in sleep: Jacob? And I answered: Here I am.
31.12: Who said: Lift up thine eyes, and see all the males ascending upon the females, them of diverse colours, the spotted and the speckled. For I have seen all things that Laban hath done to thee.
31.13: I am the God of Bethel, where thou didst anoint the stone, and didst vow the vow unto me. Now therefore arise, and go out of this land, returning into the land of thy nativity.
31.14: And Rachel and Lia answered: Have we any thing left in the goods and heritage of our father's house?
31.15: Hath he not reputed us as strangers, and sold us, and eaten up the price of us?
31.16: But God hath taken our father's riches, and delivered them to us, and to our children: wherefore do all things that God hath commanded thee.
31.17: Jacob therefore rose up, and setting his children and wives upon camels, went his way.
31.18: And he took all his substance, and flocks, and whatsoever he had gotten in Mesopotamia, and went forward to Isaac his father into the land of Chanaan.
31.19: At that time Laban was gone to shear his sheep, and Rachel stole the idols of her father.
31.20: And Jacob would not confess to his father in law that he fled.
31.21: And when he was gone as well himself as all things that were his right, & having passed the river was marching on to the Mount Galaad,
31.22: it was told Laban the third day that Jacob fled.
31.23: Who, taking his brethren unto him, pursued him seven days: and he overtook him in the Mount Galaad.
31.24: And he saw in his sleep God saying unto him: Take heed thou speak not roughly any thing against Jacob.
31.25: And Jacob had now pitched his tent in the mountain: and when he with his brethren had overtaken him, he pitched his tent in the same Mount Galaad.
31.26: And he said to Jacob: Why didst thou so, that unwitting to me thou wouldst carry away my daughters as captives with the sword?
31.27: Why wouldst thou fly without my knowledge, and not tell me, that I might have brought thee on the way with joy, and songs, and timbrels, and citterns?
31.28: Thou hast not suffered me to kiss my sons and daughters: thou hast done foolishly: now also indeed,
31.29: my hand is able to requite thee evil: but the God of your father said unto me yesterday: Take heed thou speak not any thing against Jacob roughly.
31.30: Suppose thou didst desire to go to thy friends, and hadst a longing to thy father's house, why didst thou steal my gods?
31.31: Jacob answered: In that I departed unwitting to thee, I feared lest thou wouldst take away thy daughters by force.
31.32: But whereas thou chargest me with theft; with whom soever thou shalt find thy gods, let him be slain before our brethren: search whatsoever of thy things thou shalt find with me, and take away. Saying this, he knew not that Rachel had stolen the idols.
31.33: Laban therefore having gone into the tent of Jacob, and of Lia, and of both the handmaids, found them not. And when he was entered into Rachel's tent,
31.34: she in haste hid the idols under the camel's litter, and sat thereupon: and when he had sought all the tent, and found nothing,
31.35: she said: Let not my lord be angry that I cannot rise up before thee, because according to the custom of women it is now chanced to me. So his carefulness in seeking was deluded.
31.36: And Jacob being angry said in chiding manner: For what fault of mine, and for what offence of my part hast thou so chased after me,
31.37: and searched all my household-stuff? What hast thou found of all the substance of thy house? Lay it here before my brethren, and thy brethren, and let them judge between me and thee.
31.38: Have I therefore been with thee twenty years? Thy ewes and goats were not barren, the wethers of thy flock I did not eat:
31.39: neither that which the beast had caught did I shew to thee, I made good all the damage: whatsoever perished by theft thou didst exact it of me:
31.40: day and night was I parched with heat, and with frost, & sleep did fly from mine eyes.
31.41: And in this sort have I served thee in thy house twenty years, fourteen for thy daughters, and six for thy flocks: thou hast changed also my wages ten times.
31.42: Unless the God of my father Abraham, and the fear of Isaac had holpen me, peradventure now thou hadst sent me away naked: God beheld my affliction and the labour of my hands, and rebuked thee yesterday.
31.43: Laban answered him: The daughters are mine and the children, and thy flocks, and all things that thou seest are mine: what can I do to my daughters, and nephews?
31.44: Come therefore, let us enter in league: that it may be for a testimony between me and thee.
31.45: Jacob therefore took a stone, and erected it for a title:
31.46: and he said to his brethren: Bring hither stones. Who gathering them together made a heap, and they did eat upon it:
31.47: Which Laban called the witness-heap: and Jacob called the hillock of testimony, either of men according to the property of his language.
31.48: And Laban said: This heap shall be a witness between me and thee this day, and therefore the name thereof was called Galaad, that is, The witness-heap.
31.49: Our Lord behold and judge us when we shall be departed one from the other,
31.50: if thou shalt afflict my daughters, and if thou bring in other wives over them; none is witness of our talk but God, who is present and beholdeth.
31.51: And he said again to Jacob: Behold this heap, and the stone which I have erected between me and thee,
31.52: shall be a witness: this heap, I say, and the stone, be they for a testimony, if either I shall pass beyond it going towards thee, or thou shalt pass beyond it, thinking harm to me.
31.53: The God of Abraham, and the God of Nachor judge between us, the God of their father, Jacob therefore sware by the fear of his father Isaac:
31.54: and after he had offered victims in the mountain, he called his brethren to eat bread. Who when they had eaten, lodged there.
31.55: but Laban arising in the night, kissed his sons, and daughters, and blessed them: and returned unto his place.

32.1: Jacob also went on his journey that he had begun: and the Angels of God met him.
32.2: Whom when he had seen, he said: These are the Camps of God, and he called the name of that place Mahanaim, that is, Camps.
32.3: And he sent also messengers before him to Esau his brother into the land of Seir, into the country of Edom:
32.4: and he commanded them, saying: Thus speak ye unto my lord Esau: This saith thy brother Jacob: I have sojourned, and have been with Laban until this present day.
32.5: I have oxen, & asses, & sheep, & men servants: & women servants: & now I send a legacy to my lord, that I may find grace in thy sight.
32.6: And the messengers returned to Jacob, saying: We came to Esau thy brother, & behold he cometh with speed to meet thee with four hundred men.
32.7: Jacob feared exceedingly: and being sore afraid divided the people that was with him, the flocks also and the sheep, and the oxen, and the camels, into two troops,
32.8: saying: If Esau come to one troop, and strike it, the other troop that remaineth shall be saved.
32.9: And Jacob said: O God of my father Abraham, and God of my father Isaac; O Lord that didst say to me: Return into thy land, and into the place of thy nativity, and I will do thee good:
32.10: I am inferior to all thy mercies, and thy truth that thou hast fulfilled to thy servant. With my staff I passed over this Jordan: and now with two troops I do return.
32.11: Deliver me from the hand of my brother Esau, because I am sore afraid of him: lest perhaps he come, and strike the mother with the children.
32.12: Thou didst say that thou wouldst do good to me, and dilate my seed as the sand of the sea, which for multitude cannot be numbered.
32.13: And when he had slept there that night, he separated of those things which he had, gifts to his brother Esau,
32.14: she goats two hundred, he goats twenty, ewes two hundred, and rams twenty,
32.15: thirty milch camels with their colts, forty kine, and twenty bulls, twenty she asses, and their foals ten.
32.16: And he sent by the hands of his servants every flock by it-self, and he said to his servants: Go before me, and let there be a space between flock and flock.
32.17: And he commanded the former, saying: If thou meet my brother Esau, and he ask thee, whose art thou? Or whither goest thou? Or whose are these that thou doest follow?
32.18: Thou shalt answer: Jacob's thy servant, he hath sent them for gifts to my lord Esau: himself also cometh after us.
32.19: In like manner he gave commandments to the second, and the third, and to all that followed the flocks, saying: With the self-same words speak ye to Esau, when you shall find him.
32.20: And ye shall add: Jacob also thy servant himself followeth on after us; for he said: I will pacify him with the gifts that go before, and afterward I will see him, perhaps he will be gracious unto me.
32.21: The gifts therefore went before him, but himself lodged that night in the camp.
32.22: And when he was risen early, he took his two wives, and his handmaids as many, with his eleven sons, and passed over the ford Iaboc.
32.23: And when he had set over all things that appertained to him,
32.24: he tarried alone: and behold a man wrestled with him till morning.
32.25: Who when he saw that he could not overcome him, he touched the sinew of his thigh, and forthwith it shrank.
32.26: And he said to him: Let me go for it is break of day. He answered: I will not let thee go, unless thou bless me.
32.27: He therefore said: What is thy name? He answered: Jacob.
32.28: But he, no, thy name, quoth he, shall not be called Jacob. But Israel: for if thou hast been strong against God, how much more shalt thou prevail against men?
32.29: Jacob asked him: Tell me by what name art thou called? He answered: Why dost thou ask my name? and blessed him in the same place.
32.30: And Jacob called the name of the place Phanuel, saying: I have seen God face to face, and my soul was made safe.
32.31: And immediately the sun rose to him, after that he was past Phanuel; but he halted on his foot.
32.32: For which cause the children of Israel eat not the sinew, that shrunk in Jacob's thigh, unto this present day: because he touched the sinew of his thigh, and it shrunk.

33.1: And Jacob lifting up his eyes, saw Esau coming, and with him four hundred men: and he divided the children of Lia, and of Rachel, and of the two handmaids:
33.2: and he put both the handmaids and their children foremost: and Lia and her children in the second place: and Rachel and Joseph last.
33.3: And himself going forward adored prostrate to the ground seven times, until his brother came near.
33.4: Esau therefore running to meet his brother, embraced him: and clasping him fast about the neck, and kissing him wept.
33.5: And casting up his eyes he saw the women and their little ones, and said: What mean these? And do they pertain to thee? He answered: They are the little ones which God hath given to me thy servant.
33.6: And the handmaids and their children coming near bowed themselves.
33.7: Lia also with her children came near: and when they had adored in like manner, last Joseph and Rachel adored.
33.8: And Esau said: What are the troops that I did meet? He answered: That I might find grace before my lord.
33.9: But he said: I have plenty, my brother, be thy things to thyself,
33.10: And Jacob said: Do not so, I beseech thee, but if I have found grace in thine eyes, take a little present at my hands: for so have I seen thy face as if I should have seen the countenance of God: be gracious to me,
33.11: and take the blessing which I have brought thee, and which God hath given me, who giveth all things. Scarce at his brother's great instance taking it,
33.12: he said: let us march on together, and I will accompany thee in thy journey.
33.13: And Jacob said: My lord thou knowest that I have with me little ones, and sheep, and kine with young: which if I cause to overlabour themselves in going, in one day all the flocks will die.
33.14: It may please my lord to go before his servant: and I will follow softly after him, as I shall see my little ones to be able, until I come to my lord in Seir.
33.15: Esau answered: I beseech thee, that of my people at the leastwise, which is with me, there may remain some to accompany thee in the way. It is not needful, said he, this only I have need of, that I may find grace (my lord) in thy sight.
33.16: Esau therefore returned that day the same way that he came, into Seir.
33.17: And Jacob cometh into Socoth: where having built a house, and pitched his tents, he called the name of that place Socoth, that is, Tabernacles.
33.18: and he passed into Salem a city of the Sichimites, which is in the land of Chanaan, after he returned from Mesopotamia of Syria: and he dwelt beside the town.
33.19: And he bought that part of the field, wherein he had pitched his tents, of the children of Hemor, the father of Sichem, for an hundred lambs.
33.20: And erecting an altar there, on it he called upon the most Mighty God of Israel.

34.1: And Dina the daughter of Lia went forth to see the women of that country.
34.2: Whom when Sichem had seen, the son of Hemor the Hevite, the Prince of that land, he was in love with her: and he took her away, and lay with her, by force ravishing the virgin.
34.3: And his soul was fast knit unto her, and whereas she was sad, he comforted her with sweet words.
34.4: And going to Hemor his father, he said: Take me this wench to be my wife.
34.5: Which when Jacob had heard, his sons being absent and occupied in feeding of the cattle, he held his peace till they returned.
34.6: And when Hemor, Sichem's father was come forth to speak unto Jacob,
34.7: behold his sons came out of the field; and hearing what had passed, they were passing wrath, because he had done a foul thing in Israel, and committed an unlawful fact in ravishing Jacob's daughter.
34.8: Hemor therefore spake to them: The soul of my son Sichem is fastened to your daughter: Give her unto him to wife:
34.9: and let us contract marriages one with another: give us your daughters, and take you our daughters.
34.10: And dwell with us: the land is at your commandment, till, occupy, and possess it.
34.11: Yea and Sichem also said to her father and to her brethren: Let me find grace in your sight: and what soever you shall appoint I will give:
34.12: raise the dowry, and require gifts, and I shall gladly give what you shall demand: only give me this wench to wife.
34.13: Jacob's sons answered Sichem & his father in guile, being wrath for the deflowering of their sister:
34.14: We cannot do that which you demand, nor give our sister to an uncircumcised person; which with us is an unlawful & abominable thing.
34.15: But in this order we may be considerate, if you will be like to us, and all the man sex among you be circumcised:
34.16: then will we give and take mutually your daughters, and ours: and we will dwell with you, and will be one people:
34.17: but if you will not be circumcised, we will take our daughter, and depart.
34.18: The offer pleased Hemor and Sichem his son:
34.19: neither did the young man make delay, but forthwith fulfilled that which was demanded: for he loved the wench exceedingly, and he was the greatest man in all his father's house.
34.20: And going into the gate of the city, they spake to the people:
34.21: These men are men of peace, and are willing to dwell with us: let them occupy in the land, and till it, which being large and wide doth lack men to till it: their daughters we shall take to wife, and ours we will give to them.
34.22: One thing there is for the which so great a good is deferred: If we circumcise our men sex, following the rite of the nation.
34.23: And their substance, and cattle, and all things that they possess, shall be ours: only in this let us condescend, and dwelling together we shall make one people.
34.24: And they all assented, and circumcised all the man sex.
34.25: And behold the third day, when the grief of the wounds is most painful: Jacob's two sons Simeon and Levi, the brothers of Dina, taking their swords, entered into the city boldly: and killing all the man sex,
34.26: murdered withal Hemor and Sichem, taking away Dina their sister out of Sichem's house.
34.27: When they were gone forth, the other sons of Jacob ran in upon them that were slain, and spoiled the city in revenge of the rape.
34.28: And wasting all things that were in their houses and fields, their sheep and herds, and asses,
34.29: their little ones also, and their wives they led away captive.
34.30: Which things when they had boldly achieved, Jacob said to Simeon and Levi: You have troubled me, and made me odious to the Chananites, and Pherezites, the inhabiters of this land. We are few: they being gathered together will strike me; and I and my house shall be destroyed.
34.31: They answered: What? Should they abuse our sister as a strumpet?

35.1: In the mean time God spake to Jacob: Arise, and go up to Bethel, and dwell there, and make an altar to God that appeared to thee when thou didst fly from Esau thy brother.
35.2: And Jacob having called together all his house, said: Cast away the strange gods that are among you, and be cleansed and change your garments.
35.3: Arise, and let us go up into Bethel, that we may make there an altar unto God: who herd me in the day of my tribulation, and accompanied me in my journey.
35.4: They gave to him therefore all the strange gods that they had, and the earlets which were in their ears: but he buried them under the terebinth that is behind the city of Sichem.
35.5: And when they were departed, the terror of God invaded all the cities round about, and they durst not pursue them going away.
35.6: And Jacob came to Luza, which is in the land of Chanaan, surnamed Bethel: he and all the people that was with him.
35.7: And he builded there an altar, and called the name of that place, The house of God: for there God appeared to him when he fled from his brother.
35.8: The same time died Debora the nurse of Rebecca, and was buried at the foot of Bethel under an oak: and the name of that place was called The oak of weeping.
35.9: And God appeared again to Jacob after he returned from Mesopotamia of Syria, and he blessed him,
35.10: saying: Thou shalt not be called any more Jacob, but Israel shall be thy name. And he called him Israel,
35.11: and said to him: I am God Almighty, increase thou and multiply: Of thee shall be nations and peoples of nations, kings shall come forth of thy loins.
35.12: And the land which I gave to Abraham and Isaac, I will give to thee, and to thy seed after thee.
35.14: But he erected a title of stone in the place where God had spoken unto him: offering upon it liquid offerings, and pouring oil on it,
35.15: and calling the name of that place, Bethel.
35.16: And being gone forth from thence, he came in the spring time to the land which leadeth to Ephrata: wherein when Rachel was in travail,
35.17: because of difficulty in her travail, she began to be in danger, and the midwife said unto her: Fear not, for thou shalt have also this a son.
35.18: And her soul departing for pain, and death now at hand, she called the name of her son Benoni, that is the son of my pain: but his father called him Benjamin, that is the son of the right hand.
35.19: Rachel therefore died, and was buried in the high way that leadeth to Ephrata, this same is Bethleem.
35.20: And Jacob erected a title over her sepulchre: This is the title of Rachel's monument, until this present day.
35.21: Departing thence, he pitched his tent beyond the Flock tower.
35.22: And when he dwelt in that country, Ruben went, and slept with Bala his father's concubine: which thing he was not ignorant of. And the sons of Jacob were twelve.
35.23: The sons of Lia: Ruben the first begotten, and Simeon, and Levi, and Judas, and Issachar, and Zabulon.
35.24: The sons of Rachel: Joseph and Benjamin.
35.25: The sons of Bala Rachel's handmaid: Dan and Nephthali.
35.26: The sons of Zelpha Lia's handmaid: Gad and Aser: these are the sons of Jacob, that were born to him in Mesopotamia of Syria.
35.27: He came also to Isaac his father in Mambre, the city of Arbee, this is Hebron: wherein Abraham and Isaac sojourned.
35.28: And the days of Isaac were complete an hundred eighty years.
35.29: And spent with age he died, and was put to his people, being old and full of days, and Esau and Jacob his sons buried him.

36.1: And these are the generations of Esau, the same is Edom.
36.2: Esau took wives of the daughters of Chanaan: Ada the daughter of Elon the Hetheite, and Oolibama the daughter of Ana daughter of Sebeon the Heveite:
36.3: Basemath also the daughter of Ismael sister of Nabajoth.
36.4: And Ada bare Eliphaz: Basemath bare Rahuel:
36.5: Oolibama bare Jehus and Ihelon and Core, these are the sons of Esau, that were born to him in the land of Chanaan.
36.6: And Esau took his wives and sons and daughters, and every soul of his house, and his substance, and cattle, and all that he could have in the land of Chanaan: and he went into another country, and departed from his brother Jacob.
36.7: For they were exceeding rich, and could not dwell together: neither was the land of their peregrination able to bear them, for the multitude of flocks.
36.8: And Esau dwelt in Mount Seir, he is Edom.
36.9: And these are the generations of Esau the father of Edom in mount Seir,
36.10: and these are the names of his sons: Eliphaz the son of Ada the wife of Esau: Rahuel also the son of Basemath his wife.
36.11: And Eliphas had sons: Theman, Omar, Sepho, and Gathan, and Cenes.
36.12: And Thamna was the concubine of Eliphas the son of Esau: which bare to him Amalech. These are the sons of Ada the wife of Esau.
36.13: And the sons of Rahuel were Nahath and Zara, Samma and Meza. These were the sons of Basemath the wife of Esau.
36.14: These also were the sons of Oolibama, the daughter of Ana, the daughter of Sebeon, the wife of Esau, which she bare to him, Jehus, and Ihelon, and Core.
36.15: These were Dukes of the sons of Esau: the sons of Eliphaz the first-begotten of Esau: Duke Theman, Duke Omar, Duke Sepho, Duke Cenes,
36.16: Duke Core, Duke Gatham, Duke Amalech, these are the sons of Eliphaz in the land of Edom, & these are the sons of Ada.
36.17: These also were the sons of Rahuel, the son of Esau: Duke Nahath, Duke Zara, Duke Samma, Duke Meza. And these be the Dukes of Rahuel, in the Land of Edom: these be the sons of Basemath the wife of Esau.
36.18: And these were the sons of Oolibama the wife of Esau: Duke Jehus, Duke Ihelon, Duke Core. These be the Dukes of Oolibama, the daughter of Ana, and wife of Esau.
36.19: These are the sons of Esau, and these are the Dukes of them: the same is Edom.
36.20: These are the sons of Seir the Horreite, the inhabiters of the land: Lotan, and Sobal, and Sebeon, and Ana,
36.21: and Dison, and Eser, and Disan. These are Dukes of the Horreite, the sons of Seir in the Land of Edom.
36.22: And Lotan had sons: Hori and Heman. And the sister of Lotan was Thamna.
36.23: And these were the sons of Sobal: Alvan and Manahat, and Ebal, and Sepho, and Onam.
36.24: And these were the sons of Sebeon: Aia and Ana. This is Ana that found the hot waters in the wilderness, when he fed the asses of Sebeon his father:
36.25: and he had a son Dison, and a daughter Oolibama.
36.26: And these were the sons of Dison: Hamdan, and Eseban, and Iethram, and Charan.
36.27: These also were the sons of Eser: Balaan, and Zavan, and Acan.
36.28: And Disan had sons: Hus, and Aram.
36.29: These were Dukes of the Horreites: Duke Lotan, Duke Sobal, Duke Sebeon, Duke Ana,
36.30: Duke Dison, Duke Eser, Duke Disan: these were Dukes of the Horreites that ruled in the Land Seir.
36.31: And the kings that ruled in the land of Edom, before that the children of Israel had a king, were these:
36.32: Bela the son of Beor, and the name of his city Denaba.
36.33: And Bela died, and Jobab the son of Zara of Bosra reigned in his stead.
36.34: And when Jobab was dead, Husam of the land of the Themans reigned in his stead.
36.35: He also being dead, there reigned in his stead Adad the son of Badad, that struck Madian in the country of Moab: and the name of his city was Avith.
36.36: And when Adad was dead, there reigned for him Semla of Masreca.
36.37: He also being dead, Saul of the river Rohoboth, reigned in his stead.
36.38: And when he also was dead, Balanan the son of Achobor succeeded into the Kingdom.
36.39: This men also being dead Adar reigned in his place, and the name of his city was Phau: and his wife was called Meetabel, the daughter of Matred, daughter of Mezaab.
36.40: These therefore be the names of the Dukes of Esau in their kindreds, and places, and callings: Duke Thamna, Duke Alva, Duke Jetheth,
36.41: Duke Oolibama, Duke Ela, Duke Phinon,
36.42: Duke Cenez, Duke Theman, Duke Mabsar,
36.43: Duke Magdiel, Duke Hiram: these are the Dukes of Edom dwelling in the land of their Empire, the same is Esau the father of the Idumeans.

37.1: And Jacob dwelt in the land of Chanaan, wherein his father sojourned.
37.2: And these are his generations: Joseph when he was sixteen years old, fed the flock with his brethren being yet a boy: and he was with the sons of Bala and Zelpha his father's wives: and he accused his brethren to his father of a most wicked crime.
37.3: And Israel loved Joseph above all his sons, because he had begotten him in his old age and he made him a coat of diverse colours.
37.4: And his brethren seeing that he was loved of his father more than all his sons, they hated him, neither could they speak any thing to him peaceably.
37.5: It chanced also that he reported to his brethren a dream that he had seen, which occasion was the seed of great hatred.
37.6: And he said to them: hear my dream which I have seen:
37.7: I thought we bound sheaves in the field: and my sheaf arose as it were, and stood, and your sheaves standing about did adore my sheaf.
37.8: His brethren answered: What? Shalt thou be our king? Or shall we be subject to thy dominion? This occasion of his dreams and words ministered nourishment to the envy and hatred.
37.9: He saw also another dream, which telling his brethren, he said: I saw in a dream, as it were the sun, and the moon, and eleven stars adore me.
37.10: Which when he had reported to his father and brethren, his father rebuked him, and said: What meaneth this dream that thou hast seen? Why, shall I and thy mother, and thy brethren adore thee upon the earth?
37.11: His brethren therefore envied him: but his father considered the thing with himself.
37.12: And when his brethren abode in Sichem feeding their father's flocks,
37.13: Israel said to him: Thy brethren feed sheep in Sichem: come, I will send thee to them. Who answering,
37.14: I am ready; he said to him: Go, and see if all things be well with thy brethren and the sheep, and bring me word again what they do. Being sent therefore from the Vale of Hebron, he came to Sichem:
37.15: and a man found him there wandering in the field, and asked what he sought.
37.16: But he answered: I seek my brethren, shew me where they feed the flocks.
37.17: And the man said to him: They are departed from this place: for I heard them say: Let us go into Dothain. Joseph therefore went forward after his brethren, and found them in Dothain.
37.18: Who when they had seen him a far off, before he came nigh them, they devised to kill him:
37.19: and spake among themselves: Behold the dreamer cometh,
37.20: come, let us kill him, and cast him into an old cistern: and we will say a naughty wild beast hath devoured him: and then it shall appear what his dreams do profit him.
37.21: And Ruben hearing this, endeavoured to deliver him out of their hands, and said:
37.22: Do not take away his life, neither shed ye blood: but cast him into this cistern that is in the wilderness, and keep your hands harmless: and he said this, desirous to deliver him out of their hands, and to restore him to his father.
37.23: As soon therefore as he came unto his brethren, forthwith they stripped him out of his side coat, and of diverse colours,
37.24: and cast him into the old cistern, that had not water.
37.25: And sitting to eat bread, they saw Ismaelites wayfaring men coming from Galaad, and their camels carrying spices, and rosen, and myrrh into Ægypt.
37.26: Judas therefore said to his brethren: What availeth it us if we kill our brother, and conceal his blood?
37.27: It is better that he be sold to the Ismaelites, and that our hands be not polluted: for he is our brother and our flesh. His brethren assented to his words.
37.28: And when the Madianite merchants passed by, they drawing him out of the cistern, sold him to the Ismaelites, for twenty pieces of silver, who brought him into Ægypt.
37.29: And Ruben returning to the cistern, findeth not the boy:
37.30: and renting his garments went to his brethren, and said: The boy doth not appear, and whither shall I go?
37.31: And they took his coat, and dipped it in the blood of a kid which they had killed,
37.32: sending some that should carry it to their father, and should say: This we have found: see whether it be thy son's coat, or no.
37.33: Which when the father acknowledged, he said: It is my son's coat, a naughty wild beast hath eaten him, a beast hath devoured Joseph.
37.34: And tearing his garments, did on sackcloth, mourning his son a great time.
37.35: And all his children being gathered together to assuage their father's sorrow, he would not take comfort, but said: I will descend unto my son into hell, mourning. And whilst he persevered in weeping,
37.36: the Madianites sold Joseph in Ægypt to Putiphar an Eunuch of Pharao's, master of the soldiers.

38.1: The same time Judas going down from his brethren, turned in to a man an Odolamite, named Hiras.
38.2: And he saw there the daughter of a man of Chanaan, called Sue: and taking her to wife, he did company with her.
38.3: Who conceived, and bare a son, and called his name Her.
38.4: And conceiving a child again, she called her son after he was born, Onan.
38.5: She bare also the third, whom she called Sela: after whose birth, she ceased to bear any more.
38.6: And Judas gave a wife to Her his first-begotten, named Thamar.
38.7: Also Her the first-begotten of Judas, was wicked in the sight of our Lord, and was slain of him.
38.8: Judas therefore said to Onan his son: Company with thy brother's wife, and be joined to her, that thou mayest raise seed to thy brother.
38.9: He knowing that the children should not be born to himself, companying with his brother's wife, shed his seed upon the ground, lest children might be born in his brother's name.
38.10: And therefore our Lord struck him, because he did a detestable thing.
38.11: For the which cause Judas said to Thamar his daughter in law: Be a widow in thy father's house, till Sela my son grow up: for he feared lest he also might die, as his brethren. Who went her way and dwelt in her father's house.
38.12: And after many days were come and gone, the daughter of Sue the wife of Judas died. Who after his mourning having received consolation, went up to the shearers of his sheep, himself and Hiras his shepherd of his flock, the Odolamite, into Thamnas.
38.13: And it was told Thamar that her father in law came up into Thamnas to shear his sheep.
38.14: Who putting off the garments of her widowhood, took a veil: and changing her habit, sat in the cross way that leadeth to Thamnas: because Sela was grown, and she had not taken him to her husband.
38.15: Whom when Judas had seen, he supposed her to be an harlot: for she had covered her face, lest she should be known.
38.16: And going unto her, he said: Suffer me to lie with thee: for he knew her not to be his daughter in law. Who answering: What wilt thou give me that thou mayest enjoy my company?
38.17: He said: I will send thee a kid out of the flocks. And when she said again: I will suffer that thou wilt, if thou give me a pledge, till thou send that which thou dost promise;
38.18: Judas said: What wilt thou to be given thee for a pledge? She answered: Thy ring, and bracelet, and staff which thou holdest in thy hand. The woman therefore by once companying conceived,
38.19: and rising she went her way: and putting off the apparel which she had taken, put on the garments of her widowhood.
38.20: And Judas sent a kid by his shepherd the Odolamite, that he might receive the pledge again, which he had given to the woman: who when he had not found her,
38.21: he asked the men of that place: Where is the woman that sat in the cross way? All making answer: There was no harlot in this place,
38.22: he returned to Judas, and said to him: I have not found her: yea the men also of that place said unto me, that there never sat harlot there.
38.23: Judas said: Let her take it to her, surely she cannot charge us with a lie, I sent the kid which I promised: and thou didst not find her.
38.24: And behold after three months they told Judas, saying: Thamar thy daughter in law hath played the harlot, and her belly seemeth to swell. And Judas said: Bring her forth that she may be burnt.
38.25: Who when she was led to execution, she sent to her father in law, saying: By that man, whose these things are, have I conceived: look whose the ring is, and the bracelet, and the staff.
38.26: Who acknowledging the gifts, said: She is juster than I: because I did not give her to Sela my son. But he knew her no more.
38.27: And when she was ready to be brought to bed, there appeared twins in her belly: and in the very delivery of the infants, one put forth the hand, wherein the midwife tied a scarlet string, saying:
38.28: This shall come forth the former.
38.29: But he drawing back his hand, the other came forth: and the woman said: Why is the partition divided for thee? And for this cause called his name Phares.
38.30: Afterward his brother came forth, in whose hand was the scarlet string: whom she called Zara.

39.1: Therefore Joseph was brought into Ægypt, and Putiphar an Eunuch of Pharao, Prince of his army, a man of Ægypt bought him, at the hand of the Ismaelites, by whom he was brought.
39.2: And our Lord was with him, and he was a man that in all things did prosperously: and he dwelt in his master's house,
39.3: who knew very well that our Lord was with him, and that all things which he did were directed by him in his hand.
39.4: And Joseph found grace before his master, and ministered to him: by whom being made ruler over all his things, he governed the house committed to him, and all things that were delivered unto him:
39.5: and our Lord blessed the house of the Ægyptian for Joseph's sake, and multiplied as well in houses, as in lands all his substance.
39.6: Neither knew he any other thing, but the bread which he did eat. And Joseph was of beautiful countenance, and comely favoured to behold.
39.7: After many days therefore his Mistress cast her eyes on Joseph, and said: Sleep with me.
39.8: Who in nowise assenting to that wicked act, said to her: Behold, my master having delivered all things unto me, knoweth not what he hath in his own house:
39.9: Neither is there any thing which is not in my power, or that he hath not delivered to me, beside thee, that art his wife: how therefore can I do this wicked thing, and sin against my God?
39.10: With these kind of words day by day both the woman was importune upon the young man, and he refused the adultery.
39.11: And it chanced on a certain day, that Joseph went into the house, and did some business without any man with him:
39.12: and she catching the skirt of his garment, said: Sleep with me. Who leaving the cloak in her hand, fled, and went forth abroad.
39.13: And when the woman saw the garment in her hands, and herself to be contemned,
39.14: she called to her the men of her house, and said to them: See he hath brought in an Hebrew to delude us: he came upon me for to lie with me; and when I had cried out,
39.15: and he heard my voice, he left the cloak that I held, and fled forth.
39.16: For an argument therefore of her credit she reserved the cloak, & shewed it to her husband returning home,
39.17: and said: There came unto me the Hebrew servant, whom thou didst bring hither, for to delude me:
39.18: and when he heard me cry, he left the cloak which I held, and fled forth.
39.19: His master hearing these things, and giving over light credit to his wife's words, was very wrath:
39.20: and delivered Joseph into prison, where the king's prisoners were kept, and he was there shut up.
39.21: And our Lord was with Joseph, and having mercy upon him gave him grace in the sight of the chief of the prison.
39.22: Who delivered in his hand all the prisoners that were kept in custody: and whatsoever was done, was under him.
39.23: Neither did himself know anything, having committed all things to him: for our Lord was with him, and directed all his works.

40.1: These things being so done, it chanced that two Eunuchs, the cup-bearer of the king of Ægypt, and his baker, offended against their lord.
40.2: And Pharao being wrath against them (for the one was chief of the cup-bearers, the other chief baker)
40.3: he sent them into the prison of the captain of the soldiers, in the which Joseph also was prisoner.
40.4: But the keeper of the prison delivered them to Joseph, who also ministered to them: some little time was passed, and they were kept in custody.
40.5: And they saw each of them both a dream in one night, according to an interpretation agreeing to themselves:
40.6: to whom when Joseph was entered in the morning, and saw them sad,
40.7: he asked them, saying: Why is your countenance sadder today than it was wont?
40.8: Who answered: We have seen a dream, and there is nobody to interpret it to us. And Joseph said to them: Why doth not interpretation belong to God? Tell me what you have seen.
40.9: The chief of the cup-bearers first told his dream: I saw before me a vine,
40.10: wherein were three branches, growing by little and little into buds, and after the blossoms, the grapes waxed ripe:
40.11: and the cup of Pharao in my hand: and I took the grapes, and wrung them into the cup which I held, and I gave the cup to Pharao.
40.12: Joseph answered: This is the interpretation of the dream: The three branches, are yet three days:
40.13: after the which Pharao will remember thy service, & will restore thee to thy old degree: & thou shalt give him the cup according to thine office, as before thou hadst wont to do.
40.14: Only remember me, when it shall be well with thee, and do me this mercy, to put Pharao in mind that he take me out of this prison:
40.15: because I was taken away by stealth, out of the land of the Hebrews, and here an innocent was I cast into the lake.
40.16: The master of the bakers seeing that he had wisely resolved the dream, he said: And I also saw a dream, that I had three baskets of meal upon my head:
40.17: and that in one basket that was the higher, I carried all meats that are made by the art of baking, and that the birds did eat out of it.
40.18: Joseph answered: This is the interpretation of the dream: The three baskets, are yet three days:
40.19: after the which Pharao will take thy head from thee, and hang thee on the cross, and the fowls shall tear thy flesh.
40.20: The third day after this was the birth-day of Pharao: who making a great feast to his servants, at the banquet he remembered the master of the cup-bearers, and the chief of the bakers.
40.21: And he restored the one into his place, to reach him the cup,
40.22: the other he hanged on a gibbet, that the truth of the interpreter might be approved.
40.23: And yet notwithstanding the chief of the cup-bearers, prosperous things succeeding, forgat his interpreter.

41.1: After two years Pharao saw a dream. He thought he stood upon a river,
41.2: out of the which came up seven kine, fair and fat exceedingly: and they fed in marish places.
41.3: Other seven also came up out of the river, foul, and carrion lean: and they fed on the very bank of the river, in green places:
41.4: and they devoured them that had the marvelous beauty and good state of bodies. Pharao after he waked,
41.5: slept again, and saw another dream: Seven ears of corn grew forth upon one stalk full and fair:
41.6: there sprang also other ears as many, thin and blasted with adustion,
41.7: devouring all the beauty of the former. Pharao awaking up after his rest,
41.8: and when morning was come being frighted with fear, he sent to all the interpreters of Ægypt, and to all the wise men: and they being called for, told them his dream, neither was there any that could interpret it.
41.9: Then at length the master of the cup-bearers remembering himself, said: I confess my sin:
41.10: The king being angry with his servants, commanded me and the chief of the bakers to be cast into the prison of the Captain of the soldiers:
41.11: where in one night both of us saw a dream portending things to come.
41.12: There was there a young man an Hebrew, servant to the same Captain of the soldiers, to whom telling our dreams,
41.13: we heard whatsoever afterward the event of the thing proved to be so. For I was restored to my office, and he was hanged upon a gibbet.
41.14: Forthwith at the king's commandment, Joseph being brought out of the prison they polled him: and changing his apparel, brought him unto him.
41.15: To whom he said: I have seen dreams, and there is not any that can expound them; which I have heard, thou dost most wisely interpret.
41.16: Joseph answered: Without me, God shall answer prosperous things to Pharao.
41.17: Pharao therefore told that he had see: Me thought I stood upon the bank of the river,
41.18: and seven kine came up out of the bank of the river, exceeding fair, and full of flesh: which grazed on green places in a marish pasture.
41.19: And behold there followed these other seven kine, so passing ill favoured and lean, that I never saw the like in the land of Ægypt;
41.20: which having devoured and consumed the former,
41.21: gave no token of their fullness: but with the like leanness & deformity looked heavily. Awaking, and fallen again into a deep sleep,
41.22: Isaw a dream: Seven ears of corn grew forth upon one stalk, full and very fair.
41.23: Other seven also thin and blasted with adustion, sprang of the stalk,
41.24: which devoured the beauty of the former: I told the dream to the conjecturers, and there is no man that can declare it.
41.25: Joseph answered: The king's dream is one: God hath shewed to Pharao the things that he will do.
41.26: The seven fair kine, and the seven full ears, be seven years of plentifulness and both contain the self-same meaning of the dream.
41.27: Also the seven lean and thin kine, that came up after them, and the seven thin ears, and blasted with the burning wind, are seven years of famine to come.
41.28: Which shall be fulfilled in this order.
41.29: Behold there shall come seven years of great fertility in the whole Land of Ægypt:
41.30: after which shall follow other seven years of so great sterility, that all the abundance before shall be forgotten: for the famine shall consume all the land,
41.31: and the greatness of the scarcity shall destroy the greatness of the plenty.
41.32: And in that thou didst see the second time a dream pertaining to the same thing, it is a token of the certainty for that the word of God shall come to pass, and be fulfilled speedily.
41.33: Now therefore let the king provide a wise man and industrious, & make him Ruler over the Land of Ægypt:
41.34: that he may appoint overseers over all countries: and gather into barns the fifth part of the fruits, during the seven years of the fertility,
41.35: that now presently shall ensue: and let all the corn be laid up under Pharao's hands, and let it be reserved in the cities.
41.36: And let it be in a readiness against the famine of seven years to come, which shall oppress Ægypt, and the land shall not be consumed with scarcity.
41.37: The counsel pleased Pharao, and all his servants:
41.38: and he spake to them: Can we find such another man, that is full of the spirit of God?
41.39: He said therefore to Joseph: Because God hath shewed thee all things that thou hast spoken, can I find a wiser and one like unto thee?
41.40: Thou shalt be over my house, & at the commandment of thy mouth all the people shall obey: only in the throne of the Kingdom I will go before thee.
41.41: And again Pharao said to Joseph: Behold, I have appointed thee over the whole land of Ægypt.
41.42: And he took his ring from his own hand, and gave it into his hand: and he put upon him a silk robe, and put a chain of gold about his neck.
41.43: And he made him go up into his second chariot, the crier proclaiming that all should bow their knee before him, and that they should know he was made Governor over the whole Land of Ægypt.
41.44: And the king said to Joseph: I am Pharao: without thy commandment no man shall move hand or foot in all the land of Ægypt.
41.45: And he turned his name, & called him in the Ægyptian tongue the Saviour of the world. And he gave him to wife Aseneth the daughter of Putiphar Priest of Heliopolis. Joseph therefore went forth to the land of Ægypt
41.46: (and he was thirty years old when he stood in the sight of king Pharao) and did circuit all the countries of Ægypt.
41.47: And the fertility of the seven years came: and the corn being bound up into sheaves was gathered into the barns of Ægypt.
41.48: All the abundance also of grain was laid up in every city.
41.49: And there was so great abundance of wheat, that it became equal to the sand of the sea, and the plenty exceeded measure.
41.50: And there were born unto Joseph two sons before the famine came: whom Aseneth the daughter of Putiphar Priest of Heliopolis bare him.
41.51: And he called the name of the first-begotten Manasses, saying: God made me to forget all my labours, and my father's house.
41.52: The name also of the second he called Ephraim, saying: God hath made me to increase in the land of my poverty.
41.53: Therefore when the seven years of the plentifulness, that had been in Ægypt were passed:
41.54: the seven years of scarcity began to come, which Joseph foretold: and in the whole world the famine prevailed, but in all the land of Ægypt there was bread.
